Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 33 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 1.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Let me check the current state. The player moved RIGHT and is now inside the right charcoal box. Let me see how many pixels the right charcoal has now.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        child_colors = [next((x for x in seg['nodes'] if x['id'] == cid), None)['color'] for cid in n['children']]
        print(f"Green: boundary={n['boundary']} child={child_colors}")
    elif n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] < 50:
        print(f"Charcoal: pixels={n['pixels']} boundary={n['boundary']}")

# ASCII right panel
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\nRight panel:")
for i in range(26, 35):
    print(f"Row {i}: {ascii_lines[i][40:55]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see something very important! The right charcoal box has 16 pixels - it regenerated! The player moved RIGHT into the center of the right charcoal box, and the charcoal box regenerated to its full 16 pixels.
Looking at the right panel ASCII:
Row 26: `wwwwcccccwwwwwg` - top edge: ccccc (5 cells)
Row 27: `wwwwcNNNcwwwwwg` - c at col 44, player NNN at cols 45-47, c at col 48
Row 28: `wwwwcNWNcwwwwwg` - c at col 44, player NWN at cols 45-47, c at col 48
Row 29: `wwwwcNNNcwwwwwg` - c at col 44, player NNN at cols 45-47, c at col 48
Row 30: `wwwwcccccwwwwwg` - bottom edge: ccccc (5 cells)
So the right charcoal box is a complete 5x4 hollow rectangle again (16 pixels), with the player inside it.
This confirms that charcoal boxes regenerate when the player moves into their center! The player ate pixels from the edge, then moved into the center, and the charcoal box regenerated.
So the pattern is:
1. Charcoal boxes start at 16 pixels (full hollow rectangle)
2. When the player overlaps with the edge, pixels are eaten
3. When the player moves into the center, the charcoal box regenerates to 16 pixels
This means the goal is NOT to eat all pixels from the charcoal boxes, because they regenerate.
So what is the goal? Let me think about this differently.
Maybe the goal is related to the B box. The B box was launched when the player passed through it. The B box ate 1 pixel from the right charcoal box. But now the right charcoal box has regenerated to 16 pixels.
Wait, but the B box is no longer in the right panel. After the MOUSE click, the player and B box swapped positions. The B box is now in the left panel (at cols 12-14), but its center changed from B to c (charcoal).
Hmm, so the B box is no longer a B box - it's just a green box with a charcoal center. The player is now in the right panel.
Maybe the goal is to have the player in a specific position, or to have the charcoal boxes in a specific state.
Let me think about what's unique about the current state:
- Player is inside the right charcoal box (which has 16 pixels, fully regenerated)
- The "B box" in the left panel has a charcoal center (not B)
- The left charcoal box has 13 pixels
Maybe the goal is to have both charcoal boxes fully regenerated (16 pixels each)? The right one is at 16, the left one is at 13.
To regenerate the left charcoal box, I need to move the "B box" (which is now in the left panel) into the center of the left charcoal box. But I can't control the B box directly - I can only control the player.
Wait, but I can use MOUSE to swap the player and B box again! Let me try clicking on the B box (now at cols 12-14 in the left panel) to swap back.
Actually, let me first check if the B box is still clickable. The B box's center is now 'c' (charcoal), not 'B' (black). So maybe it's no longer a B box and can't be clicked.
Let me try clicking on the green box at cols 12-14 to see if it swaps.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try clicking on the green box in the left panel (former B box position)
# to see if we can swap back
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 31, 'col': 13}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
